aliases: Baked Rice with Chicken and Mushroom

english: Baked Rice with Chicken and Mushroom

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Singaporean oven baked rice with chicken, mushroom and sauce; closest match is the general baked rice dish class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers rice dishes broadly; no specific code exists for oven-baked rice with chicken and mushroom in cream, tomato or curry sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for oven baked rice dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: cream sauce or tomato sauce or curry sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of oven baked rice is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served in Singaporean Western-style cafes and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Oven Baked Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Oven Baked Rice (cr_id G00010454). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for a comparable baked rice dish with chicken and cream sauce (USDA NDB 58417) as a secondary reference for saturated fat fraction only.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice, cooked (GI ~72) adjusted for mixed dish with protein, fat and fibre
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean oven baked rice with chicken, mushroom and sauce. Estimated GI of 65 (medium) derived from University of Sydney GI Database reference for cooked white rice (GI 72) attenuated by the presence of protein (6.5 g/100 g), fat (4.21 g/100 g), dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g) and sauce matrix, consistent with published mixed-dish GI attenuation literature (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr, 2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 65 × 75.57 g (300 g × 25.19 g/100 g) / 100 = 49.1, rounded to 48.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 693 mg sodium, representing ~30% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g per serving and request reduced-salt preparation; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K : 231 mg Na per 100 g) partially offsets the sodium load, but cumulative daily sodium from other meals must be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with estimated GI 65 (medium); a 300 g serving delivers approximately 75.6 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~49.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to 150–200 g per serving to keep the glycemic load manageable; the dietary fibre content (3.7 g/100 g) and protein (6.5 g/100 g) help moderate postprandial glucose rise, but sugary or starchy sauce variants (curry with added sugar, sweet tomato sauce) should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 4.21 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~1.6 g/100 g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g by HPB; oven-baking avoids additional frying fats.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oven baked rice is a relatively low-fat preparation suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease when portion-controlled to 200–250 g; choose cream-free sauce variants (tomato or light curry) to further reduce saturated fat intake, and pair with a vegetable side to increase dietary fibre and potassium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Oven-baking avoids advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation associated with deep frying; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g supports gut microbiome immune modulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Oven baked rice is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; those sensitive to nightshades should monitor response when the tomato sauce variant is chosen, and pairing with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as turmeric or olive oil is encouraged.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oven Baked Rice is a popular Western-style rice dish widely served in Singaporean food courts, school canteens, and casual dining establishments. It consists of cooked white rice combined with chicken pieces and mushrooms, topped with a choice of cream, tomato, or curry sauce, then baked in an oven until the top is lightly golden and the sauce is bubbling. The texture is soft and moist with a slightly caramelised surface layer, and the flavour profile varies by sauce choice: rich and creamy, tangy and savoury, or mildly spiced. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium, sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database. The estimated glycemic index is 65 (medium), attenuated from plain white rice by the protein, fat, and fibre content of the dish. Clinically, the moderate sodium level (231 mg/100 g) requires portion awareness for individuals with hypertension, while the moderate carbohydrate load and medium GI necessitate portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The low total fat and oven-baked preparation method make it a relatively heart-friendly choice compared to fried rice dishes. Oven Baked Rice is a culturally hybrid dish reflecting Singapore's Western-influenced café cuisine tradition, commonly consumed at lunch and dinner.
common_user_queries: How many calories in oven baked rice?
Is oven baked rice suitable for diabetics?
Is oven baked rice high in sodium?
What is the GI of baked rice?
Can I eat oven baked rice if I have hypertension?
What sauce is used in oven baked rice?
How much protein is in oven baked rice?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010454, confidence 0.95 per source metadata). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy comparable entry as a secondary reference. GI is an analogous estimate documented with methodology. All mandatory multilingual fields populated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.6 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for a comparable baked rice dish with chicken and cream sauce; HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific oven baked rice preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB source). 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white rice reference (GI 72) attenuated for mixed-dish protein, fat, and fibre content; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in approved sources. 5. Halal status assumed based on typical Singaporean food court preparation; kosher status not confirmed. 6. Allergen profile may vary by sauce variant chosen (cream sauce contains dairy; curry sauce may contain gluten from thickeners).
